revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Jūzenji]][[ja:十禅師]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Jūzenji]][[ja:十禅師]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Jūzenji]][[ja:十禅師]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Jūzenji]][[ja:十禅師]]


{{Short description|Japanese god}}

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| image = Juzenji.png
| script_name = 十禅師
| god_of = male homosexuality
}}
'''Jūzenji''' (十禅師) is Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|qid=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} and [[Enryaku-ji]].<ref name="UH-Press-2021">{{Cite web |date=2021-10-05 |title=Rage and Ravage: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 3 |url=https://uhpress.hawaii.edu/title/rage-and-ravage-gods-of-medieval-japan-volume-3/ |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=UH Press |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="Porath-2022">{{Cite journal |last=Porath |first=Or |date=August 2022 |title=Japan's Forgotten God: Jūzenji in Medieval Texts and the Visual Arts |journal=Religions |language=en |volume=13 |issue=8 |pages=693 |doi=10.3390/rel13080693 |issn=2077-1444 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2013-01-01 |title=The Cultic World of the Blind Monks: Benzaiten, Jūzenji, and Shukujin |url=https://brill.com/view/journals/jrj/2/2-3/article-p171_4.xml |journal=Journal of Religion in Japan |language=en |volume=2 |issue=2–3 |pages=171–194 |doi=10.1163/22118349-12341254 |issn=2211-8349}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=VMPEA: Or Porath {{!}} Department of Art History |url=https://arthistory.uchicago.edu/happenings/events/vmpea-or-porath |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=arthistory.uchicago.edu}}</ref><ref>https://web.archive.org/web/20220526004029id_/https://eprints.soas.ac.uk/37277/1/Sala_2022.pdf</ref> He was seen as a god of male homosexuality.<ref name="Porath-2022" /> and sometimes seen as being the core god which all things emanated from.<ref name="Porath-2022" />

He is sometimes seen as having a close relationship with {{ill|Shuten-dōji|en|Shuten-dōji|qid=Q986527|lt=Shuten-dōji|1=Shuten-dōji}}.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=198}} This is emphasized with his link to white monkeys, the {{ill|Shinshi|en|Shinshi|qid=Q10444973|lt=Shinshi|1=Shinshi}} of {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=198}}

He was worshipped as one of the seven key emanations of [[Sannō Gongen|Sanno Gongen]].<ref name="Porath-2022" /> He was at times seen as being the core god which all things emanated from.<ref name="Porath-2022" />

He is sometimes identified with [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref name="Porath-2022" /><ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=215-216}} and at other times with [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]].<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=215-216}} or {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=206-207}} or as a masculine  [[Benzaiten]].<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=216-218}}

His name is derived from a group of ten Buddhist monks, which was a prominent institution in the Heian period, but declined by the time his worship began.<ref name="Faure-2020">{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2020 |title=Jūzenji, ou l'enfance du divin |url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/asie_0766-1177_2020_num_29_1_1551 |journal=Cahiers d'Extrême-Asie |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=265–288 |doi=10.3406/asie.2020.1551|s2cid=235055852 }}</ref> It means "Ten Masters of Practice".<ref>{{Cite web |title=Reevaluating Mountain Practice in Premodern Japan |url=https://csr.princeton.edu/events/2021/reevaluating-mountain-practice-premodern-japan |access-date=2023-11-21 |website=Center for Culture, Society, and Religion |language=en}}</ref>

== History ==
{{ill|Jien|en|Jien|qid=Q715993|lt=Jien|1=Jien}} is said to have possibly had a homosexual love affair with Juzenji.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=198}} In one myth every night he came to Jien in the form of a monkey and sat on his shoulder.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=216-218}} He was seen as a god of male homosexuality.<ref name="Porath-2022" />

Juzenji is a complex deity seen as ambivalent both curing and blessing people. He is normally depicted as a youth.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=205}}<ref name="Porath-2022" /> He is compared to {{ill|Janus|en|Janus|qid=Q167685|lt=Janus|1=Janus}} in his ambivalent nature.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=223}}

{{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} chroniclers like [[Kōshū (chronicler)|Kōshū]] have written about Jūzenji. They keep his complex nature in their writings. They avoid simplifying him into just a symbol.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=205}}

The "[[Sange yōryakki]]", written by [[Gigen (monk)|Gigen]] during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} tells of [[Saichō|Saichō's]] first climb on {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} in 785. There, [[Saichō]] met a divine youth. This youth called himself the deity of destiny. He also said he was the divine child and {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|qid=Q269515|lt=Dōshōjin|1=Dōsojin}}.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=206-207}}

{{ill|Genshin|en|Genshin|qid=Q1009263|lt=Genshin|1=Genshin}}, was a famous monk. He lived from 942 to 1017. His teachings describe Jūzenji. He said Jūzenji was centrally linked to the forces of the universe.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=206-207}}

The Sannō Mitsuki offers a different view. It says Jūzenji has a dual role in Heaven and Earth. He is {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō|1=Ākāśagarbha}} in heaven. On earth, Jūzenji is {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō|1=Kṣitigarbha}}.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=206-207}}

The Sange Sairyakki is another text. It talks about Saichō's meeting with the divine youth. This youth had three names. The first is Dōshōten. This means he was born with all beings. The second is Yugyōjin. He oversees the destiny of beings. The third is Jūzenji. He shares Zen's bliss and creates karmic ties.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=206-207}}

The Sefurisan engi adds to the story. It suggests the youth [[Saichō]] met was Oto Gohō. Oto Gohō later protected Saichō on his trip to China.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=206-207}}

He was sometimes identified as a serpentine deity linked to placenta and identified with [[Benzaiten]].<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=216-218}}

His popularity declined in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=223}}, and worship ended with the [[Meiji Restoration]].<ref name="Faure-2020" /> His shrine at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|qid=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}} was renamed as Jugeguu.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8883 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 }}</ref>

==Link to Prince Shotoku==
Jūzenji and Shōtoku Taishi share similarities. Shōtoku is often shown as a child. He represents the bodhisattva [[Kannon|Kannon]]. In 1069, a ritual at [[Hōryū-ji|Hōryūji]] focused on Shōtoku as a seven-year-old. This was different from the usual worship of Guze Kannon. This choice reflects the period's emphasis on youth. It was a time when puppet emperors were under {{ill|Cloistered Emperor|en|Cloistered Emperor|qid=Q3053200|lt=cloistered emperors|1=Cloistered Emperor}}' control. It was also when {{Nihongo|Young Prince|若宮|Wakamiya}} deities became popular.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=215-216}} Such deities were generally linked to [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]].<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=186}}

{{ill|Jien|en|Jien|qid=Q715993|lt=Jien|1=Jien}}, a poet, revered both [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]] and Jūzenji. He saw them as almost the same. Jien made many offerings at the Jūzenji shrine. In 1216, he dedicated a vow to Jūzenji and Shōtoku's mausoleum after a dream.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=215-216}}

== Relationship with Daigyōji ==
Daigyōji is considered Jūzenji's uncle. He is known as an "evil god." He represents Japan's original [[Landlord deity|landlord deities]]. By the thirteenth century, Daigyōji had a zoomorphic appearance. He is an avatar of [[Sarutahiko Ōkami|Sarutahiko]]. Some medieval stories replace [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] with Jūzenji. The connection between Jūzenji and Daigyōji is explained in the Rō no miko ki. This text is about the origins of Jūzenji shrine mediums, known as the rō no miko.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=215-216}} Sarutahiko and Daigyoji also have monkey associations.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=216-218}}

==Artistic depiction==
Jūzenji is not often shown in art. When shown, he is usually a young or middle-aged monk.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=207-212}}

In the Sannō [[mandala]]s, Jūzenji appears with other deities. One description shows him as a monk over twenty years old. He holds a scroll and a fan. Another source describes him as a respected monk in dreams.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=207-212}}

One painting shows Jūzenji on a platform. A small monkey is climbing stairs towards him. Jizō is above him in a golden circle. The background shows rocks, trees, and seven stars. These stars represent the seven upper [[Hie Shrine|Hie shrines]]. Monks from {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}} mostly worshipped this form.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=207-212}}

Recently, images of Jūzenji as a youth have been found. There are a few examples. They show Jūzenji with similar features to {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. He has a wish-fulfilling jewel and a unique banner holder on a lotus stalk.<ref name="UH-Press-2021" />{{Rp|page=207-212}}

==In popular culture==
The shrine of Juzenji is a setting in {{ill|The Tale of the Heike|en|The Tale of the Heike|qid=Q830740|lt=The Tale of the Heike|1=The Tale of the Heike}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Yoshikawa |first=Eiji |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Heike-Story-Translation-Classic-Japanese/dp/4805310448 |title=The Heike Story: A Modern Translation of the Classic Japanese Tale of Love and War |last2=Bhowmik |first2=Davinder |date=2011-04-10 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-4-8053-1044-1 |edition=Reprint |language=en |translator-last=Uramatsu |translator-first=Fuki Wooyenaka}}</ref>{{Rp|page=60}}

==See also==
* [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]]
* [[Hachiman]]
